title: Disorder-driven symmetry suppression by van der Waals planar defects in a magnetic topological insulator
summary: Magnetic topological insulators offer a platform to control electronic topology through magnetic order, yet reliable routes to tune their properties remain limited. Here, we show that ion irradiation allows to modify the magnetic and the topological properties of the van der Waals magnetic topological insulator MnBi$_2$Te$_4$. Using inert ion beams, intrinsic defects are introduced via collision cascades without chemical doping. We identify two distinct regimes. At low fluence, cation antisite disorder leads to a near-complete redistribution of Bi over cation sites while preserving long-range crystallographic order, accompanied by a transition from $p$-type to $n$-type transport. At high fluence, cation-anion intermixing drives the formation of a previously unreported layer-disordered phase characterized by a high density of van der Waals-specific planar defects, including swapped bilayers. Despite significant structural disorder, the system retains partial periodic order up to high displacement levels. Magnetometry and X-ray spectroscopy show that the Mn high-spin state and antiferromagnetic interactions persist, while magnetic anisotropy is strongly reduced. At the same time, the anomalous Hall response is suppressed by over an order of magnitude, far exceeding the change in magnetization, indicating a direct modification of Berry curvature. These results establish ion irradiation as a means to tune topology through defect engineering and reveal a disorder-driven approach to control symmetry and electronic structure in van der Waals magnetic materials.

AI.tldr: This study demonstrates ion irradiation as an effective, dopant-free method to engineer defects in the van der Waals magnetic topological insulator MnBi₂Te₄. By controlling irradiation fluence, the team achieves tunable carrier type and suppresses anomalous Hall effect far beyond what magnetization reduction alone would predict, suggesting Berry curvature can be directly manipulated through defect engineering in magnetic topological materials.
AI.motivation: Magnetic topological insulators can theoretically control electronic topology via magnetic order, but practical methods to reliably tune their properties remain scarce.
AI.method: Inert ion beam irradiation introduces intrinsic defects via collision cascades at two regimes: low fluence induces cation antisite disorder and Bi redistribution; high fluence drives cation-anion intermixing forming a layer-disordered phase with van der Waals-specific planar defects. Properties are probed via magnetometry and X-ray spectroscopy.
AI.result: At low fluence, p-to-n type transport transition occurs via Bi site redistribution while maintaining crystallographic order. At high fluence, a novel layer-disordered phase forms with persistent Mn high-spin state and antiferromagnetism despite reduced anisotropy. Critically, anomalous Hall response drops >10x—far exceeding magnetization changes—indicating direct Berry curvature modification.
AI.conclusion: Ion irradiation emerges as a powerful defect-engineering strategy for tuning topology and symmetry in van der Waals magnetic materials, opening pathways to manipulate Berry curvature independently of magnetization.

title: Chemical Origins of Non-Bonded Interactions Within and Between Solids
summary: Non-bonded interactions govern structure, stability, and function across a wide range of solid-state materials, yet their chemical origins are often difficult to resolve from total energies alone. Here we generalize absolutely localized molecular orbital energy decomposition analysis to quantify and interpret non-bonded interactions within and between solids at the density functional theory level. Across molecular crystals, moiré heterobilayers, and layered perovskite heterostructures, this framework separates lattice-formation energies, interlayer binding energies, and band-structure changes into chemically intuitive contributions from frozen interactions, polarization, and charge transfer. The analysis reveals how dispersion controls polymorph stability in pharmaceutical crystals, how local stacking modulates interlayer coupling in MoS2/WSe2, and how alkali-cation substitution switches the quantum-well character of layered perovskite heterostructures. By connecting emergent solid-state properties to microscopic interaction mechanisms, this framework provides a chemically transparent basis for understanding and designing complex materials.

AI.tldr: A new ALMO-EDA framework extends energy decomposition analysis to solid-state systems, revealing how frozen interactions, polarization, and charge transfer contribute to lattice formation, interlayer binding, and band structures across molecular crystals, 2D heterobilayers, and perovskite heterostructures.
AI.motivation: Non-bonded interactions critically govern structure, stability, and function of solid-state materials, but their chemical origins are difficult to resolve from total energies alone. A chemically transparent analysis method was needed.
AI.method: Generalized absolutely localized molecular orbital (ALMO) energy decomposition analysis at the DFT level, which separates lattice-formation energies, interlayer binding energies, and band-structure changes into contributions from frozen interactions, polarization, and charge transfer.
AI.result: Three key applications demonstrated: (1) dispersion controls polymorph stability in pharmaceutical crystals, (2) local stacking modulates interlayer coupling in MoS2/WSe2 moiré heterobilayers, and (3) alkali-cation substitution switches quantum-well character in layered perovskite heterostructures.
AI.conclusion: This ALMO-EDA framework provides a chemically intuitive decomposition of non-bonded interactions in solids, enabling connection of emergent material properties to underlying interaction mechanisms. It offers a powerful tool for understanding and designing complex solid-state materials.

title: Reversible nanoscale patterning of WTe$_2$ with a scanning tunneling microscope
summary: Manipulating the lattice structure of ferroelectric quantum materials enables their use in low-power electronic devices, including field-effect transistors. WTe$_2$ is a Weyl-semimetal candidate and ferroelectric, both properties arising from the reduced crystal symmetry of its T$_\mathrm{d}$ ground state. The T$_\mathrm{d}$ crystal phase results from a Peierls distortion of the 1T parent structure and an interlayer shift. While experiments in WTe$_2$ have established ferroelectric switching and transient control of the predicted topological phase via ultrafast excitations, persistent electronic changes on the nanometer scale remain elusive. Here, we demonstrate that current pulses applied via scanning tunneling microscopy can both write and erase persistent nanometer-scale patterns on the surface of WTe$_2$. These patterns consist of apparent picometer in-plane and out-of-plane atomic displacements, accompanied by changes to the local density of states. The out-of-plane displacements further modulate the Peierls-like distortion present in WTe$_2$, while the in-plane displacements are indicative of ferroelectric switching. The induced patterns can be repositioned and erased, suggesting a nanoscale handle on the ferroelectric properties of WTe$_2$.

title: p-Wave Orbital Angular Momentum Texture in a Chiral Crystal
summary: The spin and orbital angular momentum (SAM and OAM) are conceptually analogous, yet their roles in condensed matter systems have not been often treated on equal footing. While SAM has been extensively explored, OAM has long been regarded as quenched in crystalline environments and thus largely overlooked. Recent experimental and theoretical advances, however, have demonstrated that OAM can drive a variety of novel electronic phenomena, highlighting the importance of probing OAM textures in the electronic band structure. Here, we investigate the momentum-space OAM texture of (TaSe4)2I, a one-dimensional chiral crystal. Using circular-dichroism angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (CD-ARPES), we uncover a p-wave OAM texture accompanied by OAM dipole structures. This orbital p-wave texture is intimately connected to, and thus controllable by the chirality of the host lattice. Complementary spin-resolved ARPES measurements and first-principles calculations reveal that the OAM polarization overwhelmingly dominates the low-energy electronic properties of (TaSe4)2I, far exceeding the SAM polarization. These observations represent the experimental verification of a new type of OAM texture in crystalline materials. Most importantly, these findings underscore a promising material platform for spinless orbitronics applications and lay the foundation for realizing multipolar OAM textures-orbital counterparts of the spin texture in unconventional magnets.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates orbital angular momentum (OAM) textures in the 1D chiral crystal (TaSe4)2I using CD-ARPES, discovering a p-wave OAM texture that dominates the material's electronic properties over spin angular momentum (SAM). This represents experimental verification of a new OAM texture type, providing a platform for spinless orbitronics and multipolar OAM textures.
AI.motivation: While SAM has been extensively studied, OAM has been long considered quenched in crystals and thus overlooked, despite being conceptually analogous to SAM. Recent advances show OAM can drive novel electronic phenomena, motivating investigation of OAM textures in electronic band structures.
AI.method: CD-ARPES (circular-dichroism angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy), complemented by spin-resolved ARPES and first-principles calculations
AI.result: A p-wave OAM texture with dipole structures was discovered in (TaSe4)2I. The OAM polarization dominates low-energy electronic properties, far exceeding SAM polarization. This OAM texture is controllable by the chirality of the host lattice.
AI.conclusion: These findings establish (TaSe4)2I as a promising material platform for spinless orbitronics and provide a foundation for realizing multipolar OAM textures—the orbital counterparts of spin textures in unconventional magnets.

title: Non-Relativistic Spin-Orbit Interaction in Triplet Superconductors: Edelstein Effect and Spin Pumping by Electric Fields
summary: Non-relativistic momentum-dependent spin splitting, as observed in collinear altermagnets and non-collinear $p$-wave magnets, provides exciting avenues for controlling spin dynamics. Here, we reveal a distinct form of non-relativistic ``spin-orbit coupling" in triplet superconductors by demonstrating that the triplet order parameter induces a wave-vector-dependent spin texture of Bogoliubov quasiparticles, thereby entangling their orbital and spin motions. Even in the absence of relativistic spin-orbit coupling, this intertwining of spin and orbital motion allows an electric field to generate spin polarization in a $p$-wave superconductor -- that is, an Edelstein effect. Building on this mechanism, we propose an efficient scheme for the nonlinear generation of a DC spin current via electric near fields, driven by AC spin polarization and electron velocity. This general principle offers a powerful route for generating and manipulating spin currents in unconventional superconductors.

AI.tldr: Triplet superconductors exhibit a new form of non-relativistic spin-orbit coupling: the triplet order parameter induces a wave-vector-dependent spin texture on Bogoliubov quasiparticles, enabling an Edelstein effect and efficient nonlinear DC spin current generation using electric near fields.
AI.motivation: Non-relativistic momentum-dependent spin splitting (observed in altermagnets and non-collinear p-wave magnets) offers ways to control spin dynamics, but similar effects in superconductors remain underexplored.
AI.method: The authors demonstrate that triplet order parameters induce wave-vector-dependent spin textures of Bogoliubov quasiparticles, entangling spin and orbital motion even without relativistic spin-orbit coupling. They propose a scheme for nonlinear DC spin current generation via AC spin polarization and electron velocity driven by electric near fields.
AI.result: The triplet order parameter creates a wave-vector-dependent spin texture that enables an Edelstein effect in p-wave superconductors—allowing electric fields to generate spin polarization despite no relativistic spin-orbit coupling. A nonlinear mechanism for DC spin current generation via electric near fields is proposed.
AI.conclusion: This work establishes a general principle for generating and manipulating spin currents in unconventional superconductors through nonlinear processes driven by electric near fields.

title: Unveiling Magnetic Frustration via the Elastocaloric Effect
summary: Motivated by experimental progress in pressure and strain tuning of quantum materials, we examine the thermodynamic response of frustrated magnets to uniaxial strain. Specifically, we study Ising and Heisenberg models on spatially anisotropic triangular (and, for the Ising model, also kagome) lattices. We determine the entropy as a function of temperature and strain, and use it to compute the elastic Grüneisen ratio $η$. The Ising models can be strain-tuned into and out of classical spin-liquid phases, and we show that $η$ can become arbitrarily large at low temperature $T$ near the point of maximal frustration, a universal hallmark of an extensive ground-state entropy. In contrast, the spin-$1/2$ Heisenberg model is moderately frustrated and displays multiple $T=0$ phase transitions. These transitions dominate $η$ at low $T$ while the intermediate-$T$ behavior is similar to that of the Ising model. We discuss the extent to which the elastic Grüneisen ratio can be used to deduce the phase diagram, and we connect our results to recent experiments on triangular-lattice magnets.

AI.tldr: Study of how uniaxial strain affects thermodynamic properties of frustrated magnets on triangular/kagome lattices. Large elastic Grüneisen ratio signals extensive ground-state entropy in Ising spin liquids; Heisenberg model shows multiple quantum phase transitions dominating low-temperature behavior.
AI.motivation: Recent experimental progress in pressure and strain tuning of quantum materials has motivated examination of the thermodynamic response of frustrated magnets to uniaxial strain.
AI.method: The authors studied Ising and Heisenberg models on spatially anisotropic triangular (and kagome for Ising) lattices. They calculated entropy as a function of temperature and strain, then computed the elastic Grüneisen ratio η.
AI.result: For Ising models, strain can tune systems into and out of classical spin-liquid phases, with η becoming arbitrarily large at low T near maximal frustration—serving as a universal hallmark of extensive ground-state entropy. For the spin-1/2 Heisenberg model, which is moderately frustrated, multiple T=0 phase transitions dominate η at low T, though intermediate-T behavior resembles that of the Ising model.
AI.conclusion: The elastic Grüneisen ratio η can effectively serve as a probe to deduce the phase diagram of frustrated magnets. The results connect theoretical predictions to recent experiments on triangular-lattice magnets.

title: Local distortions as a source of piezoelectric/stiffness decoupling in B-doped AlScN
summary: We present a first-principles analysis of the wurtzite pseudo-ternary (Al,Sc,B)N to elucidate the structural origin of a decoupling between stiffness $C_{33}$ and piezoelectric response $e_{33}$ upon boron incorporation, using DFT-relaxed 100-atom special quasirandom structures across a broad composition range. Pair distribution function analysis reveals interstitial threefold-coordinated boron atoms that have displaced from the tetrahedral cation site. Direct structural analysis establishes their preferential orientation along the $c$-axis and identifies a scandium-activated creation mechanism. The vertical coordination asymmetry of each cation is quantified through a site-specific axial asymmetry ratio (AAR), showing that boron incorporation progressively symmetrizes the Sc environment. Correlation with Born effective charges demonstrates that this symmetrization is the mechanism behind the piezoelectric enhancement.

AI.tldr: This study reveals that boron incorporation in wurtzite (Al,Sc,B)N creates interstitial threefold-coordinated B atoms with c-axis preference. These atoms trigger Sc-environment symmetrization (quantified by a new Axial Asymmetry Ratio metric), which is identified as the mechanism driving the unexpected piezoelectric enhancement despite maintained stiffness. This structural insight enables rational design of advanced piezoelectric materials.
AI.motivation: The observed anomalous decoupling between elastic stiffness (C33) and piezoelectric coefficient (e33) upon boron incorporation lacks clear structural explanation, hindering rational materials design.
AI.method: DFT calculations on 100-atom special quasirandom structures across wide composition range, combined with pair distribution function analysis and site-specific axial asymmetry ratio quantification.
AI.result: Boron occupies interstitial threefold-coordinated sites displaced from tetrahedral positions, with preferential c-axis orientation created via a scandium-activated mechanism. This incorporation progressively symmetrizes the Sc environment, and this symmetrization directly correlates with enhanced piezoelectric response.
AI.conclusion: The discovered AAR metric provides a quantifiable link between local structural symmetry and piezoelectric response, offering a pathway for designing improved piezoelectric materials.

title: Observation of flat-bottom U-shaped energy gap in high-Tc nickelate (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 thin films
summary: The discovery of high transition temperature (high-Tc) superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper (R-P) bilayer nickelates under high pressure has stimulated extensive work to understand the underlying mechanism and search for superconductors with higher Tc. The recent realization of superconductivity in R-P bilayer nickelate thin films with onset Tc above 40 K at ambient-pressure enables the use of a wide array of powerful experimental tools to investigate the unconventional high-Tc superconductivity in bilayer nickelates. Here, using ultra-low temperature scanning tunneling microscopy/spectroscopy (STM/S) and electrical transport study, we report the first successful observation of an energy-symmetric, flat-bottom U-shaped gap with zero residual density of states around the Fermi level in the high-Tc nickelate (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 thin film grown on SrLaAlO4 substrate. Before and after STM/S studies, transport measurements on the same sample reveal consistent superconducting behaviors showing zero resistance, with an onset Tc above 40 K and zero resistance Tc above 20 K. The tunneling spectra exhibit highly unconventional temperature evolution, characterized by a rapid filling of the U-shaped energy gap to a V-shaped gap as the temperature increases. Furthermore, the U-shaped energy gap is reduced under a c-axis magnetic field of 14 T. The energy-symmetric U-shaped gap, taken together with its dependence on magnetic field and temperature, is consistent with the behavior of a superconducting gap, suggesting a nodeless gap function at ultra-low temperatures. Our findings shed new lights on the nature of high-Tc superconductivity and provide an encouraging and thought-provoking hint for a local superconductivity with Tc above liquid nitrogen boiling temperature in nickelate superconductors at ambient or zero pressure.

AI.tldr: Researchers report the first STM/S observation of a nodeless superconducting gap (U-shaped with zero DOS at Fermi level) in (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 bilayer nickelate thin films at ambient pressure, with onset Tc above 40 K. The gap shows conventional magnetic field suppression and unusual temperature evolution from U to V shape, providing evidence for high-Tc superconductivity and hints toward potentially achieving superconductivity above liquid nitrogen temperature.
AI.motivation: The discovery of high-Tc superconductivity in Ruddlesden-Popper bilayer nickelates under high pressure has motivated extensive research to understand the underlying mechanism. Recent success in achieving superconductivity in thin films at ambient pressure now enables the use of powerful experimental tools like STM/S to investigate the unconventional superconductivity in these materials.
AI.method: Ultra-low temperature scanning tunneling microscopy/spectroscopy (STM/S) and electrical transport measurements were performed on (La,Pr)3Ni2O7 thin films grown on SrLaAlO4 substrate. The same sample was used for both STM/S studies and transport measurements before and after characterization.
AI.result: First observation of an energy-symmetric, flat-bottom U-shaped gap with zero residual density of states at the Fermi level in high-Tc nickelate thin films. Transport measurements show zero resistance with onset Tc above 40 K and zero resistance Tc above 20 K. The tunneling spectra reveal unconventional temperature evolution from U-shaped to V-shaped gaps upon heating. The U-shaped gap is suppressed under a 14 T c-axis magnetic field.
AI.conclusion: The energy-symmetric U-shaped gap, combined with its magnetic field and temperature dependence, indicates a nodeless gap function at ultra-low temperatures. These findings advance our understanding of high-Tc superconductivity in nickelates and suggest the possibility of achieving superconductivity above liquid nitrogen boiling temperature in nickelate superconductors at ambient pressure.

title: Itinerant antiferromagnetism in the antagonistic pair compound Y$_4$Co$_3$Ag
summary: Low dimensional crystallographic motifs have long been associated with desirable physical properties. The confinement of electrons to low dimensions is thought to enhance quantum fluctuations and may promote correlated phenomena. Here, using the antagonistic pair concept, we add Y to the immiscible Co-Ag pair to discover Y$_4$Co$_3$Ag. This compound adopts a monoclinic $I$2/$m$ structure consisting of Y channels that are filled by one-dimensional zigzag and hexagonal Co chains, which extend along the crystallographic $b$-axis with no nearest neighbor contacts between Co and Ag atoms. Transport, magnetic, and specific heat measurements reveal that Y$_4$Co$_3$Ag orders antiferromagnetically at $T_N=14.9$ K with an effective magnetic moment $μ_{\text{eff}}$ = 1.4 $μ_{\text{B}}$/Co. Specific heat measurements show only a small entropy loss on the order of $0.1\,R\ln2$ associated with magnetic order, and magnetization isotherms, in DC fields up to 70 kOe at 1.8 K and in pulsed fields up to 600 kOe at 500 mK, indicate a small ordered moment of less than 0.2 $μ_B$/Co. Taken together, our results imply the presence of small, itinerant moments and strong fluctuations in Y$_4$Co$_3$Ag, suggesting that Y$_4$Co$_3$Ag may be a promising candidate material to investigate itinerant magnetic interactions in a quasi-one dimensional system.

AI.tldr: A new quasi-one-dimensional compound Y₄Co₃Ag was discovered using the antagonistic pair concept. It shows antiferromagnetic order at 14.9 K with small itinerant moments and strong fluctuations, making it a promising candidate for studying itinerant magnetism in low-dimensional systems.
AI.motivation: Low dimensional crystallographic motifs are associated with desirable physical properties due to electron confinement enhancing quantum fluctuations and promoting correlated phenomena. The study aims to discover new materials in this regime using the antagonistic pair concept to explore immiscible element combinations.
AI.method: Discovery via the antagonistic pair concept (adding Y to immiscible Co-Ag pair); Synthesis and structural characterization (monoclinic I2/m structure with Y channels containing zigzag and hexagonal Co chains along b-axis); Transport, magnetic, and specific heat measurements; Magnetization isotherms up to 600 kOe in pulsed fields at 500 mK.
AI.result: Y₄Co₃Ag exhibits antiferromagnetic ordering at T_N = 14.9 K with effective moment μ_eff = 1.4 μ_B/Co, small entropy loss (~0.1R ln2), and ordered moment < 0.2 μ_B/Co, indicating small itinerant moments and strong fluctuations characteristic of low-dimensional systems.
AI.conclusion: Y₄Co₃Ag is a promising candidate material for investigating itinerant magnetic interactions in quasi-one-dimensional systems, combining the antagonistic pair concept with low-dimensional crystallography to access regimes of strong quantum fluctuations.

title: Benchmarking empirical and machine-learned interatomic potentials using phase diagram predictions for Lead
summary: We compare the predicted phase behaviour of lead (Pb) using three different interatomic potential models, including an embedded atom method (EAM), a modified embedded atom method (MEAM), and a neural network-based machine-learned model in the form of an ephemeral data-derived potential (EDDP). Using nested sampling and replica-exchange nested sampling simulations, we computed thermodynamic and structural properties at pressures up to 60 GPa, mapping both melting behaviour and solid-phase stability. Both the EAM and MEAM models predict the face-centred cubic (FCC) phase to remain stable up to approximately 60 GPa. In contrast, the EDDP model captures the experimentally-observed FCC-to-hexagonal close-packed (HCP) transition at around 15 GPa. These results highlight the importance of training data and model flexibility in accurately describing high-pressure phase behaviour, and demonstrate the effectiveness of nested sampling as a robust framework for exploring phase stability in materials. Particularly, the combination of nested sampling with modern machine-learned interatomic potentials - delivering near ab initio accuracy at tractable cost - opens the door to truly predictive and exhaustive exploration. EDDPs trained on diverse, out-of-equilibrium configurations appear particularly well suited to this task, offering a robust and transferable framework for unbiased phase discovery.

title: Equidistant resonance jumps in superconducting coplanar resonators driven by Abrikosov vortices
summary: Superconducting coplanar resonators are key building blocks of cryogenic microwave circuits, yet their performance in perpendicular magnetic fields is ultimately limited by Abrikosov vortices. In this work we investigate the dependence of the transmission parameter $S_{21}$ of niobium quarter-wave coplanar resonators on perpendicular magnetic fields up to 40 Oe and at temperatures between 18 mK and 5 K. Beyond the reversible Meissner regime, the entire resonance peak exhibits abrupt, staircase-like jumps as a function of magnetic field. Upon reversal of the field sweep, these jumps form an almost equidistant series with spacing 1.7-1.8 Oe, which, in agreement with theoretical estimates, we interpret as signatures of multiple-vortex entry and exit events. Additionally, we observe the non-proportional responses of the resonant frequency and the internal quality factor that indicate a complex contribution of vortex and antivortex configurations. We believe that our results will stimulate further studies of large vortex-antivortex systems, explicitly accounting for their discrete nature.

AI.tldr: Study investigates how magnetic fields affect superconducting niobium coplanar resonators, discovering discrete staircase-like jumps in transmission that correspond to individual vortex penetration events, with implications for understanding vortex dynamics in microwave circuitry.
AI.motivation: Superconducting coplanar resonators are essential components of cryogenic microwave circuits, but their performance degrades in perpendicular magnetic fields due to Abrikosov vortex penetration, limiting practical applications.
AI.method: Nb quarter-wave coplanar resonators were fabricated and characterized via S21 parameter measurements across perpendicular magnetic fields up to 40 Oe and temperatures from 18 mK to 5 K.
AI.result: Abrupt staircase-like jumps in the resonance peak were observed beyond the Meissner regime, with near-equidistant spacing of ~1.7-1.8 Oe upon field reversal—interpreted as discrete vortex entry/exit events. Both resonant frequency and internal quality factor showed non-proportional responses, indicating complex contributions from vortex-antivortex configurations.
AI.conclusion: These findings provide experimental evidence for discrete vortex entry/exit in superconducting resonators and highlight the need for explicit consideration of vortex-antivortex configurations in future theoretical models and device optimization.

title: Thermodynamic signatures of a field-induced ordered intermediate phase in Na$_2$Co$_2$TeO$_6$
summary: The honeycomb cobaltate Na$_2$Co$_2$TeO$_6$ has recently been proposed as a candidate material for hosting field-induced quantum spin liquid (QSL) behavior. Here, we present a comprehensive thermodynamic study of its low-temperature, high-field phase diagram using magnetization, specific heat, and magnetocaloric-effect measurements down to 1 K. In zero field, we observe a weak residual moment that provides further insight into the nature of the magnetic ground state. For in-plane magnetic fields ($B \parallel a^*$), we identify three field-induced transitions at $B_{c1} \simeq 6$ T, $B_{c2} \simeq 7.8$ T, and $B_{c3} \simeq 10.4$ T. The magnetic Grüneisen parameter and specific heat reveal clear thermodynamic signatures of these successive phase transitions enclosing two intermediate phases. Contrary to expectations for a field-induced QSL, the phase between $B_{c2}$ and $B_{c3}$ lacks enhanced magnetic entropy but instead shows behavior consistent with a distinct ordered state. Above $B_{c3}$, the absence of additional anomalies indicates a crossover to a conventional spin-polarized regime. Our results place stringent thermodynamic constraints on the proposed QSL scenario in Na$_2$Co$_2$TeO$_6$, calling for further microscopic investigations to establish the precise nature of the field-induced phases.

AI.tldr: Study of Na₂Co₂TeO₆ reveals three field-induced transitions with two intermediate phases. Contrary to QSL expectations, the intermediate phase shows ordered state characteristics (no enhanced magnetic entropy), challenging the proposed quantum spin liquid scenario.
AI.motivation: Na₂Co₂TeO₆ was proposed as a candidate material for hosting field-induced quantum spin liquid (QSL) behavior, prompting systematic investigation of its low-temperature, high-field phase diagram to verify or refute this claim.
AI.method: Comprehensive thermodynamic study using magnetization, specific heat, and magnetocaloric-effect measurements down to 1 K temperature, focusing on the low-temperature, high-field phase diagram of the honeycomb cobaltate.
AI.result: Three field-induced transitions identified at B_c1 ≈ 6 T, B_c2 ≈ 7.8 T, and B_c3 ≈ 10.4 T. Two intermediate phases enclosed by these transitions. A weak residual moment observed in zero field. The phase between B_c2 and B_c3 shows behavior consistent with a distinct ordered state rather than QSL (lacks enhanced magnetic entropy). Above B_c3, conventional spin-polarized regime crossover with no additional anomalies.
AI.conclusion: The study places stringent thermodynamic constraints on the proposed QSL scenario in Na₂Co₂TeO₆, indicating that the intermediate phase lacks QSL characteristics (no enhanced magnetic entropy). The absence of anomalies above B_c3 suggests a crossover to conventional spin-polarized regime. Further microscopic investigations are needed to establish the precise nature of the field-induced phases.

title: Interfacial Reconstructions and Engineering in III-V@II-VI Core-Shell Quantum Dots
summary: In core/shell quantum dots (QDs), the interface between semiconductors of different chemical character largely determines their optoelectronic properties. In III-V/II-VI systems, this boundary involves pronounced chemical and electronic discontinuities that can generate trap states even under complete surface passivation. Using density functional theory on atomistic models of InAs/CdSe QDs, we systematically reconstruct atomic arrangements at the surface and interface to evaluate how local coordination and interfacial dipoles influence the electronic structure. Abrupt interfaces induce charge imbalance and band-gap collapse, whereas introducing an alloyed interlayer that mixes core and shell atoms and vacancies restores energetic alignment and yields delocalized band-edge states, consistent with experimental findings. We also introduce a charge-flow analysis that quantifies charge redistribution across the QD, providing a framework for realistic modeling of interlayer formation and predictive design of defect-free interfaces in core@shell architectures.

AI.tldr: This DFT study on InAs/CdSe quantum dots reveals that abrupt core/shell interfaces create problematic charge imbalance and band-gap collapse, which can be resolved by introducing an alloyed interlayer that smooths the electronic discontinuity and enables optimal optoelectronic properties.
AI.motivation: In III-V/II-VI core/shell QDs, the interface between chemically different semiconductors creates trap states due to pronounced chemical and electronic discontinuities, even when surface passivation is complete. Understanding and controlling interface properties is critical for optoelectronic performance.
AI.method: Density functional theory on atomistic models of InAs/CdSe QDs, with systematic reconstruction of atomic arrangements at surface and interface, plus a novel charge-flow analysis to quantify charge redistribution.
AI.result: Abrupt interfaces cause charge imbalance and band-gap collapse. An alloyed interlayer mixing core and shell atoms with vacancies restores energetic alignment and produces delocalized band-edge states—consistent with experimental observations.
AI.conclusion: An alloyed interlayer approach restores proper energetic alignment and yields delocalized band-edge states, offering a framework for predicting defect-free interfaces in core@shell QD architectures.

title: Effective increase of a superconducting critical temperature in a high-entropy electron mixture
summary: We show theoretically that a superconducting critical temperature can be effectively increased in a high-entropy mixture of electrons belonging to conduction and valence bands. In order to employ the entropy of mixing into the superconducting phase dynamics, we suggest to use a metallic trap that removes quasiparticle excitations from the superconductor. This makes the concentration of Cooper pairs a dynamic variable of the entropy of mixing, and thus affects the Ginzburg-Landau functional of the superconductor effectively reducing the first expansion coefficient or, in other words, increasing the critical temperature.

title: A practical Laser-Heated Diamond Anvil Cell synthesis technique and recovery workflow for metastable MnSb2 and YbZn2 phases
summary: The creation and exploration of new materials under extreme pressure-temperature conditions has become increasingly reliant on laser-heated diamond anvil cell (LHDAC) techniques, which provide direct access to previously unexplored regions of multinary phase diagrams. Whereas numerous high-pressure phases have been identified in situ, systematic recovery and post-synthesis physical property characterization of these materials remain significant challenges. In this work, we present the development of an integrated LHDAC synthesis and demonstrate a practical LHDAC-based synthesis workflow that enables stabilization and recovery of metastable intermetallic phases for subsequent structural and transport studies. Using this approach, we successfully achieved LHDAC synthesis of high-pressure MnSb2 and YbZn2 phases under moderate pressures. Synchrotron X-ray diffraction and spatial mapping confirm dominant formation of the targeted phases, whereas laboratory-based refinement quantifies phase fractions despite intrinsic microstrain and minor secondary phases. High-pressure transport measurements on recovered samples reveal tunable by pressure electronic instabilities in both systems. In MnSb2, pressure suppresses two high-temperature magnetic ordering anomalies, observed in transport, by 5 GPa and for higher pressures induces a new low-temperature feature that increases with further pressure increase. In hexagonal high-pressure YbZn2, an electronic reconstruction emerges at ~11 GPa, characterized by semiconducting-like behavior from ~ 30 K to 300 K and a broad low-temperature coherence crossover near 30 K. Our results establish LHDAC synthesis not only as a structural discovery tool, but also as an experimental platform for investigating correlated quantum states stabilized far from equilibrium thermodynamic conditions.

title: Charge-sensitive vibrational modes in BEDT-TTF salts: Signatures of charge ordering and site charge
summary: BEDT-TTF-based organic conductors host a number of ground states, tuned by electron repulsion from Mott and charge ordered insulators to superconductors. Knowing charge distribution on the molecular sites in the insulating state of these materials is a key to understanding the origin of these ground states. We survey and discuss the C=C stretching modes in BEDT-TTF based molecular conductors. These molecular vibrations are extremely crucial in characterization of charge-ordered insulators, and are recently linked to superconductivity in some compounds. Focusing on the known examples of BEDT-TTF$^{+0.5}$ salts, we analyse the reliability of the C=C stretching modes for the determination of charge ordering and absolute site charge. Considering the charge-ordered states, a prominent shift in frequency of 141 cm$^{-1}$ per elementary charge $e$ for $ν_{27}(b_{1u})$ and 98 cm$^{-1}$$e$ for $ν_2$($a_g$) can be clearly realised, however, the distribution resulting from different compounds span over 20 cm$^{-1}$. For nominal BEDT-TTF$^{+0.5}$ compounds, the distribution of the resonance also extends around 20 cm$^{-1}$, yielding an unexpected large uncertainty of $Δρ~\approx~(~\pm~0.045)e$, which is presumably due to the influence of small differences in the structure. This highlights the limitations of charge-frequency relations to detect small deviations in absolute charge values on molecular lattice sites, and emphasises on the use of the relations to estimate charge-ordering, rather than absolute site charge.

AI.tldr: Review paper critically evaluating C=C stretching mode frequencies as a probe for charge distribution in BEDT-TTF-based organic conductors. Despite established linear relationships between frequency shift and charge, practical application shows ±0.045e uncertainty due to structural variations, limiting precision for absolute charge quantification but remaining useful for qualitative charge-ordering detection.
AI.motivation: Understanding ground states (Mott insulators, charge-ordered insulators, superconductors) in BEDT-TTF organic conductors requires knowing precise charge distribution on molecular sites. Raman/IR-active C=C stretching modes have been used as experimental probes, but their accuracy for quantitative charge determination needed critical assessment.
AI.method: Survey and analysis of C=C stretching modes (molecular vibrations) across known BEDT-TTF⁺⁰·⁵ salts; comparison of frequency distributions from different compounds to assess reliability of charge-frequency calibration curves.
AI.result: Charge-frequency shifts of 141 cm⁻¹/e and 98 cm⁻¹/e were confirmed, but frequency distributions span ~20 cm⁻¹ across compounds, yielding unexpectedly large uncertainty (Δρ ≈ ±0.045e). Small structural differences between compounds account for this variability.
AI.conclusion: Despite clear frequency shifts (141 cm⁻¹/e for ν₂₇(b₁ᵤ) and 98 cm⁻¹/e for ν₂(a_g)), the practical uncertainty in determining absolute site charge reaches Δρ ≈ ±0.045e across different compounds. The authors recommend using these charge-frequency relations primarily for detecting charge-ordering patterns rather than precise quantitative charge determination.

title: Fuzzy band structure of quantum dots by Bloch Orbital Expansion, unconventional insights into geometry-electronic structure relations
summary: The extension of ab-initio methods like density functional theory (DFT) to quantum dot (QD) geometries has enabled researchers to explore relationships between QD surface termination and electronic structure. However, fully utilizing the data from DFT requires novel classification methods for QD orbitals. Here, we identify relationships between QD geometry and electronic structure by transforming real-space QD orbitals into momentum-space using Bloch orbital expansion (BOE), yielding a fuzzy QD band structure. Comparing with bulk band structures, we show that truncated, unpassivated facets in III-V and II-VI QDs produce mid-gap orbitals derived from bulk surface orbitals; an identification challenging in real space. QDs with reconstructed facets, however, feature delocalized orbitals formed by superposition of bulk Bloch orbitals. Moreover, we prepare for the first time atomistic core/shell QD models and by the BOE expansion we can clearly identify the core/shell band alignment not possible in real space. These findings emphasize BOE as a vital tool for connecting computational and experimental insights in nanocrystal research.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Bloch Orbital Expansion (BOE) as a method to analyze quantum dot electronic structure by transforming orbitals to momentum space. The technique reveals that truncated facets create mid-gap states while reconstructed facets produce delocalized orbitals, and enables unprecedented identification of core/shell band alignment in atomistic QD models.
AI.motivation: The extension of DFT to quantum dots has enabled exploration of QD surface termination effects on electronic structure, but existing methods lack effective classification techniques for QD orbitals—particularly for identifying orbital origins and band alignments.
AI.method: Bloch Orbital Expansion (BOE): transforming real-space QD orbitals into momentum-space to generate a fuzzy QD band structure for comparison with bulk band structures; analysis of atomistic core/shell QD models.
AI.result: Truncated, unpassivated facets produce identifiable mid-gap orbitals from bulk surface states; reconstructed facets yield delocalized orbitals via Bloch superposition; core/shell QD band alignment was characterized for the first time at the atomistic level.
AI.conclusion: BOE represents a crucial analytical tool that bridges computational modeling and experimental characterization of nanocrystals, enabling identification of orbital origins, surface effects, and core/shell alignments that were previously challenging or impossible to detect.

title: Spherically symmetric approaches in the theoretical study of low-dimensional magnets
summary: The main ideas and some of the most important results of the spherically symmetric self-consistent approach and a number of related theoretical algorithms are presented. These methods make it possible to study low-dimensional Heisenberg-type spin models, including frustrated ones, with careful consideration of the theoretic (Mermin-Wagner and Marshall) theorems, as well as the site spin constraint. Thus, the difficulties that may arise in the traditional analysis of low-dimensional magnetic systems are avoided. The approach can also be applied to the spin-pseudospin model, and is also embedded in more complex constructions when considering spin models with free carriers, such as the basic and three-band Hubbard models, t-J and s-d models, and the Kondo lattice.

title: Charge Transfer from Perovskite Quantum Dots to Multifunctional Ligands with Tethered Molecular Species
summary: Perovskite quantum dots (pQDs) are promising materials for optoelectronic and photocatalytic applications due to their unique optical properties. To enhance charge carrier extraction or injection donor/acceptor molecules can be tethered to the pQD. These molecules must strongly bind to the ionic surfaces of pQDs without compromising colloidal stability. These we achieve by using multifunctional ligands containing a quaternary ammonium binding group for strong pQDs surface attachment, a long tail group for colloidal stability, and a functional group near the pQD surface. Such pQDs with ferrocene-functionalized ligands show fast photoexcited hole transfer with near-unity efficiency. Density functional theory calculations reveal how ferrocene's molecular structure reorganizes following hole transfer, affecting its charge separation efficiency. This approach can also be extended to in photoexcited electron and energy transfer processes with pQDs. Therefore, this strategy offers a blueprint for creating efficient QD-molecular hybrids for applications like photocatalysis.

title: Lattice Relaxation Flattens Chern Bands in Rhombohedral Graphene Stacks
summary: Motivated by recent observations of integer and fractional Chern insulators in rhombohedral graphene stacks aligned with hexagonal boron nitride (hBN), we propose and study a model in which the moiré potential is defined by the pattern of layer-shear strain fields produced by lattice relaxation in these heterostructures. Although these strain fields decrease exponentially with the number of layers, their imprints on electrons residing away from the contact layer are non-negligible. In the absence of a displacement field, lattice relaxation effects amplify the electronic differences among the two different stackings with hBN. These differences, although attenuated at the single-electron level, survive in the so-called moiré-distant regime and are further enhanced with the inclusion of electron interactions. We find that lattice relaxation plays a crucial role in flattening and isolating a valley-polarized Hartree-Fock electron band with $|C|=1$ Chern number. Our results challenge the conventional wisdom on moiré effects in these heterostructures by illustrating the intertwined effects of long-range Coulomb interactions and lattice relaxation, and opens the door to explore different regimes of twist angles and displacement fields for the search for topological states.

AI.tldr: This paper demonstrates that lattice relaxation-generated strain fields, though exponentially decaying with layer count, significantly imprint electrons in multilayer graphene/hBN structures. By combining these strain effects with electron correlations, they achieve a topologically nontrivial |C|=1 band, challenging the view that moiré physics is confined to the contact layer.
AI.motivation: Recent experiments have observed integer and fractional Chern insulators in rhombohedral graphene/hBN stacks, but conventional theories focus on moiré effects only at the contact layer. This prompts investigation into how strain fields at all layers influence electronic structure.
AI.method: They constructed a theoretical model incorporating layer-shear strain fields from lattice relaxation into the moiré potential. They performed Hartree-Fock calculations with electron interactions included, analyzing both single-particle properties and many-body effects across different stacking configurations and displacement fields.
AI.result: They identified a valley-polarized Hartree-Fock electron band with Chern number |C|=1 that emerges from the combined effects of lattice relaxation and electron interactions. Crucially, these lattice-induced differences between stackings persist even in the moiré-distant regime and are amplified rather than washed out by correlations.
AI.conclusion: Lattice relaxation should be considered alongside Coulomb interactions when exploring topological states in moiré systems. The work broadens the parameter space for discovering Chern insulators by reconsidering the role of strain effects at all layers, not just the contact layer.

title: Long-range magnetic ordering and structural phase transition in disordered high-entropy spinel chromites
summary: High-entropy spinel oxides provide an excellent platform for investigating entropy-stabilized correlated systems with strong configurational disorder. In this work, we systematically study the temperature evolution of the structural and magnetic properties of Cr-based high-entropy spinels with compositions $(Mn_{0.2}Co_{0.2}Ni_{0.2}Cu_{0.2}Zn_{0.2})Cr_2O_4$ and $(Mg_{0.2}Co_{0.2}Ni_{0.2}Cu_{0.2}Zn_{0.2})Cr_2O_4$. Our results reveal that both systems crystallize in cubic structure with space group \textit{$Fd\overline{3}m$} at room temperature. Each system undergoes antiferromagnetic ordering below the Néel temperatures $ T_N$ = 49 K and 35 K, respectively. Neutron diffraction measurements confirm the emergence of long-range magnetic order with spiral spin arrangement. Both systems exhibit a structural phase transition from cubic \textit{$Fd\overline{3}m$} to orthorhombic \textit{Fddd} symmetry at approximately 55 K and 85 K, respectively. Notably, despite the significant chemical disorder at the A site, both systems undergo transitions analogous to those observed in low entropy spinel systems. This behavior suggests that high configurational entropy may promote global structural stabilization despite local chemical disorder, thereby preserving long-range orderings and the characteristic symmetry-breaking transitions of the pristine spinel systems.

AI.tldr: This study demonstrates that high-entropy Cr-based spinel oxides maintain structural phase transitions and magnetic ordering patterns similar to their low-entropy counterparts, suggesting entropy acts as a stabilizing force that preserves long-range order even under substantial chemical disorder.
AI.motivation: Investigate entropy-stabilized correlated systems with strong configurational disorder by studying how high-entropy spinel oxides behave structurally and magnetically compared to conventional low-entropy counterparts.
AI.method: Temperature-dependent structural and magnetic characterization of two Cr-based high-entropy spinel compositions using neutron diffraction measurements to track phase transitions and spin arrangements.
AI.result: Both high-entropy spinels crystallize in cubic Fd-3m structure at room temperature, undergo antiferromagnetic ordering below TN ≈ 49 K and 35 K with confirmed long-range spiral spin arrangements, and exhibit cubic-to-orthorhombic (Fddd) structural transitions at ~55 K and ~85 K respectively—despite significant A-site chemical disorder.
AI.conclusion: High configurational entropy promotes global structural stabilization despite local chemical disorder, preserving long-range orderings and characteristic symmetry-breaking transitions comparable to pristine spinel systems.

title: Causation-guided mechanism identification and interpretable reduced-order modeling of damage-driving grain-boundary stress in creep
summary: Grain-boundary (GB) local stress is central to the initiation and evolution of long-term creep damage in polycrystalline superalloys. Owing to the high-dimensional nonlinear relationships between the GB stress response and multiple crystallographic, microstructural, and micromechanical characteristics, it remains challenging to identify the key characteristics governing GB stress and to elucidate their mechanisms of influence. Dislocation-climb-affected crystal-plasticity finite-element simulations of minimal grain clusters are combined with an integrated causation-guided machine-learning framework, in which mechanics-informed descriptors are analyzed by causation entropy to identify governing mechanisms and then distilled into a reduced-order regression form for interpretable prediction of GB normal stress. Among 18 physically motivated characteristics, the GB inclination angle, the slip transmission, the climb-related Schmid-type indicator, and the elastic-modulus mismatch are found to be dominant, revealing the coupled roles of interfacial geometry, crystallographic compatibility, creep stress relaxation, and micromechanical contrast. The identified characteristics hierarchy and functional representation remain effective under multiaxial loading and can be extended to tricrystal systems through physically interpretable nonlocal augmentation when a purely local description becomes insufficient, demonstrating strong physical consistency and robust generalizability across physical conditions. The extracted candidate functions also improve surrogate-model performance across multiple machine-learning model classes, providing supporting evidence for the physical relevance and efficiency of the identified representation. The proposed methods demonstrate strong potential for the development of interpretable machine-learning models and for the study of microscale nonlocal damage.

AI.tldr: This paper combines crystal-plasticity finite-element simulations with causation-guided machine learning to identify the key factors governing grain-boundary stress in polycrystalline superalloys. Four dominant characteristics (inclination angle, slip transmission, Schmid-type indicator, elastic mismatch) were identified from 18 candidates, revealing coupled effects of geometry, compatibility, stress relaxation, and micromechanical contrast.
AI.motivation: Grain-boundary (GB) local stress is central to long-term creep damage in polycrystalline superalloys. The high-dimensional nonlinear relationships between GB stress response and multiple crystallographic, microstructural, and micromechanical characteristics make it challenging to identify key governing characteristics and understand their mechanisms of influence.
AI.method: The authors combine dislocation-climb-affected crystal-plasticity finite-element (CPFE) simulations with an integrated causation-guided machine-learning framework. Mechanics-informed descriptors are analyzed using causation entropy to identify governing mechanisms, then distilled into a reduced-order regression form for interpretable prediction. Nonlocal augmentation is applied for tricrystal systems when local descriptions are insufficient.
AI.result: Four dominant characteristics were identified among 18 physically motivated candidates: GB inclination angle, slip transmission, climb-related Schmid-type indicator, and elastic-modulus mismatch. These reveal coupled roles of interfacial geometry, crystallographic compatibility, creep stress relaxation, and micromechanical contrast. The identified characteristics hierarchy remains effective under multiaxial loading and can be extended to tricrystal systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed causation-guided ML framework effectively identifies four dominant characteristics (GB inclination angle, slip transmission, climb-related Schmid-type indicator, and elastic-modulus mismatch) that govern GB normal stress. The framework demonstrates robust generalizability across loading conditions and geometric configurations, with extracted features improving surrogate-model performance.

title: Near-degenerate competing magnetic orders in EuAgAs: a tunable route to altermagnetism
summary: Altermagnets (AMs) have recently emerged as a distinct magnetic class bridging central features of ferromagnets (FMs) and antiferromagnets (AFMs), offering new opportunities for spin-based electronics. While they possess zero net magnetization like collinear AFMs, they simultaneously exhibit momentum-dependent spin splitting long thought exclusive to FMs. Despite intense theoretical interest, experimentally accessible materials hosting both altermagnetism and nontrivial band topology remain scarce. EuAgAs, crystallizing in space group $P6_3/mmc$, was previously identified via density functional theory (DFT) as a bulk altermagnetic Dirac semimetal. Contrary to these predictions, our neutron diffraction experiments reveal that the bulk ground state adopts a $\mathbf{q} = (0,0,\tfrac{1}{2})$ AFM structure with an in-plane $\uparrow\uparrow\downarrow\downarrow$ spin sequence. Systematic DFT calculations, however, uncover a remarkable near-degeneracy among competing magnetic orders: the FM and AM configurations lie only $0.11$ and $0.40~\text{meV/f.u.}$ above the AFM ground state, respectively. We further show that while a simple Heisenberg model favors a spin-spiral ground state, the inclusion of non-Heisenberg biquadratic coupling stabilizes the observed commensurate AFM phase. This near-degeneracy renders the magnetic state highly tunable, with DFT predicting a transition to the altermagnetic phase under hydrostatic pressure at approximately $14 \text{ GPa}$, establishing EuAgAs as a controllable platform for accessing topological altermagnetism.

AI.tldr: This study reveals a discrepancy between DFT predictions and experimental reality in EuAgAs. While initial calculations suggested altermagnetic behavior, neutron diffraction shows the ground state is actually AFM with specific spin ordering. Nevertheless, the nearly degenerate energy landscape of competing magnetic states makes this material highly tunable—specifically, ~14 GPa pressure could stabilize the desired altermagnetic phase with topological properties.
AI.motivation: Despite intense theoretical interest in altermagnets (which bridge FM and AFM characteristics), experimentally accessible materials hosting both altermagnetism and nontrivial band topology remain scarce. Previous DFT predictions suggested EuAgAs as an altermagnetic Dirac semimetal, but experimental verification of its actual magnetic ground state was needed.
AI.method: Neutron diffraction experiments to determine the bulk ground state structure, combined with systematic DFT calculations across competing magnetic configurations. Theoretical modeling using Heisenberg and extended (biquadratic coupling) spin models to understand stabilization mechanisms.
AI.result: Neutron diffraction reveals EuAgAs actually adopts a q=(0,0,½) AFM structure with ↑↑↓↓ spin sequence, contradicting initial DFT predictions. However, DFT shows remarkable near-degeneracy: FM lies only 0.11 meV/f.u. and AM only 0.40 meV/f.u. above the AFM ground state. The inclusion of non-Heisenberg biquadratic coupling (not captured by simple Heisenberg model) stabilizes the observed commensurate AFM phase. Pressure of ~14 GPa is predicted to drive transition to the altermagnetic phase.
AI.conclusion: EuAgAs represents a controllable platform for accessing topological altermagnetism. Hydrostatic pressure of ~14 GPa can induce a transition from the AFM ground state to the desired altermagnetic phase, providing a pathway to exploit both altermagnetism and nontrivial band topology in this material.

title: Thermal conductivity of seifertite and pyrite-type SiO$_2$: A comparative study
summary: Thermal conductivity is a fundamental material property that plays a crucial role in understanding the dynamics and evolution of planetary interiors. Despite its importance, the thermal conductivity of seifertite and pyrite-type SiO$_2$ remains unknown. Here, we calculate the lattice thermal conductivities of seifertite and pyrite-type SiO$_2$ using the Green-Kubo method based on molecular dynamics (MD) simulations driven by two machine learning potentials (MLPs) constructed from the SCAN and PBEsol exchange-correlation functionals, with $\textit{ab initio}$-level accuracy. To demonstrate our methodology, we also compute thermal conductivities using the phonon quasiparticle approach for comparison. Overall, the Green-Kubo method predicts up to 119 % higher thermal conductivity with a temperature dependence close to $T^{-1}$, as it fully captures diffusion-like phonons at high temperatures that are missed by the phonon quasiparticle approach. The 19 % reduction in thermal conductivity across the phase transition from seifertite to the pyrite-type phase suggests the potential formation of a thermally insulating layer in the mantle of super-Earths.

AI.tldr: First-ever calculation of thermal conductivity for seifertite and pyrite-type SiO₂ using machine learning potentials and Green-Kubo MD method; findings suggest thermally insulating layers may form in super-Earth mantles at phase transitions.
AI.motivation: Thermal conductivity of seifertite and pyrite-type SiO₂ was previously unknown but critical for understanding planetary interior dynamics and evolution
AI.method: Green-Kubo MD simulations with two machine learning potentials (SCAN/PBEsol); cross-validated with phonon quasiparticle approach
AI.result: Green-Kubo predicts up to 119% higher thermal conductivity with ~T⁻¹ temperature dependence; captures diffusion-like phonons missed by phonon quasiparticle approach
AI.conclusion: 19% thermal conductivity reduction across phase transition suggests insulating layer formation in super-Earth mantles; MLPs achieve ab initio-level accuracy for these predictions.

title: Rapid Atmospheric Vapor Deposition of H:In2O3 Transparent Conducting Oxide Thin Films
summary: Transparent conducting oxides (TCOs) are essential for the optoelectronics industry, but there is a critical gap in cost-effective methods to rapidly deposit low sheet resistance, high transmittance films without damaging delicate materials, including emerging soft semiconductors like metal-halide perovskites. In this work, atmospheric pressure chemical vapor deposition (AP-CVD) is used to synthesise H:In2O3 films with 7.20+/-0.01 Ohm/sq sheet resistance (0.50+/-0.06 mOhm.cm resistivity) and transmittance up to 89% in the near-infrared (NIR), surpassing commercial sputter-deposited indium tin oxide. The growth rate is 40x higher than atomic layer deposition (ALD), and the AP-CVD films are fully processed under atmospheric conditions at only 140 C. Comparison of secondary ion mass spectrometry and time-of-flight elastic recoil detection analysis with changes in carrier concentration indicate that H dopants are introduced from the water oxidant. There is an increase in mobility form 40+/-10 cm2/Vs to 160+/-30 cm2/Vs when changing from O2 to H2O as the oxidant, which is attributed to H dopants passivating oxygen vacancies that act as carrier scattering centers. This work establishes AP-CVD as a promising method for manufacturing high figure-of-merit TCOs in a rapid, scalable and cost-effective manner, using mild growth conditions compatible with thermally-sensitive materials.

AI.tldr: This work demonstrates AP-CVD synthesis of hydrogen-doped In₂O₃ as a high-performance, low-temperature TCO alternative. The use of H₂O as oxidant introduces H dopants that passivate oxygen vacancies, dramatically improving carrier mobility while enabling 40x faster deposition than ALD at just 140°C—establishing a scalable, cost-effective pathway for TCO manufacturing compatible with heat-sensitive materials.
AI.motivation: There is a critical need for cost-effective, rapid deposition methods for transparent conducting oxides (TCOs) that can achieve low sheet resistance and high transmittance without damaging delicate soft semiconductors, including emerging metal-halide perovskites.
AI.method: Atmospheric pressure chemical vapor deposition (AP-CVD) was used to synthesize H:In₂O₃ films using water (H₂O) as the oxidant, enabling mild processing conditions.
AI.result: Achieved H:In₂O₃ films with 7.20±0.01 Ohm/sq sheet resistance, 0.50±0.06 mOhm·cm resistivity, and 89% NIR transmittance—surpassing commercial sputtered ITO. Mobility increased from 40±10 to 160±30 cm²/Vs when switching from O₂ to H₂O oxidant due to H dopants passivating oxygen vacancy scattering centers.
AI.conclusion: AP-CVD emerges as a promising method for manufacturing high figure-of-merit TCOs with 40x faster growth than ALD, achieving superior electrical/optical properties at only 140°C—fully compatible with thermally-sensitive materials like perovskites.

title: Bridging Atomistic Simulation and Experimental Processing Timescales with Goal-Directed Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Atomic-scale modeling has advanced rapidly through integration of machine learning, yet a key bottleneck remains. Even with an accurate potential energy surface and a clear target material, we still lack a practical atomistic dynamics framework that can simulate how materials form under realistic synthesis and processing conditions. Many processing transformations are governed by rare events in non-idealized evolving environments, while direct molecular dynamics is limited by femtosecond timesteps and short accessible trajectories. Existing acceleration methods often require prior mechanistic knowledge, including reaction coordinates, collective variables, event tables, or pathway guesses, which is rarely available in real experiments. Here we present an E(3)-equivariant deep reinforcement learning framework that enables goal-directed pathway discovery without hand-crafted reaction coordinates. The framework introduces a complementary operating mode for atomistic simulation in which realistic, non-idealized environments can be addressed directly while retaining kinetic plausibility through barrier-aware rewards. As a challenging benchmark, we target silicon dry oxidation, where rare-event pathways in amorphous SiO2 are effectively inaccessible to conventional atomistic methods. We treat an O2 molecule as an agent that performs continuous rigid-body translations and rotations in a Si/a-SiO2 environment. The agent is trained with an episode-level objective that rewards verified O2 dissociation while preferring low effective activation barriers. We demonstrate that the learned policy discovers kinetically favorable O2 diffusion and dissociation pathways in a disordered Si/a-SiO2 environment, progressively improving success rate while reducing effective activation barriers over training. We also discuss how the approach can be generalized to other processing and synthesis problems.

AI.tldr: This paper presents an E(3)-equivariant deep reinforcement learning framework for goal-directed atomistic pathway discovery without hand-crafted reaction coordinates, applied to silicon dry oxidation as a benchmark—demonstrating that treating O2 as an agent enables finding kinetically favorable dissociation pathways in amorphous SiO2 environments that are otherwise inaccessible to conventional methods.
AI.motivation: Current atomistic simulation methods struggle with rare-event dynamics under realistic synthesis/processing conditions due to limitations of direct MD (femtosecond timesteps) and existing acceleration methods requiring prior mechanistic knowledge (reaction coordinates, CVs, event tables, pathway guesses) that are unavailable in real experiments.
AI.method: E(3)-equivariant deep reinforcement learning framework treating O2 molecules as agents performing continuous rigid-body translations and rotations, using an episode-level objective with barrier-aware rewards for verified dissociation while preferring low effective activation barriers.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the learned policy discovers kinetically favorable O2 diffusion and dissociation pathways in amorphous Si/SiO2 environment, with progressive improvement in success rate and reduced effective activation barriers during training.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully discovers kinetically favorable O2 diffusion and dissociation pathways in disordered Si/a-SiO2 environments, with progressive improvement in success rates and reduction in effective activation barriers over training iterations.

title: Universal Magnetic Structure Prediction from Atomic Coordinates with Near-Experimental Accuracy
summary: Magnetic order is a fundamental property of materials, governing collective behavior and enabling a broad range of functionalities. Yet magnetic structure remains difficult to determine: experiments are costly and specialized, while first-principles methods often struggle with the noncollinear and incommensurate orders found in real materials. Here we introduce magnetic structure network (MSN), an E(3) equivariant graph neural network that predicts both collinear and non-collinear magnetic structures directly from atomic crystal structures, trained directly on experimentally determined structures from MAGNDATA. By proposing the primitive modulated structure representation (PMSR), we are able to encode commensurate and incommensurate structures in a unified way without symmetry assumptions. The model achieves strong performance across all modulation components and reconstructs experimental magnetic structures with high fidelity. Our approach provides a scalable framework for rapid magnetic structure prediction and opens a route to data-driven discovery of magnetic materials.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed MSN, an E(3) equivariant graph neural network that predicts both collinear and non-collinear magnetic structures directly from atomic crystal structures, using a novel primitive modulated structure representation to handle commensurate and incommensurate structures uniformly.
AI.motivation: Magnetic structure determination is challenging due to costly experiments, specialized requirements, and limitations of first-principles methods in handling noncollinear and incommensurate orders commonly found in real materials.
AI.method: MSN is an E(3) equivariant graph neural network trained on MAGNDATA, employing the primitive modulated structure representation (PMSR) to unify encoding of commensurate and incommensurate magnetic structures without symmetry assumptions.
AI.result: The model achieves strong performance across all modulation components and reconstructs experimental magnetic structures with high fidelity.
AI.conclusion: The MSN approach provides a scalable framework for rapid magnetic structure prediction and enables data-driven discovery of magnetic materials.
